posts.title: Why Zambian SMEs Need Proactive Security Audits
posts.excerpt: Small businesses are increasingly targeted online. A structured security audit is the fastest way to protect reputation and customer trust.
posts.body:
Many small and medium enterprises in Zambia rely on digital tools for sales, payments, and customer communication — but few have a formal security review process.

A proactive audit helps identify weak passwords, outdated software, exposed network ports, and risky third-party integrations before attackers exploit them.

In this article I outline practical steps any business can take to start improving security without a large budget.

posts.title: Building Secure Web Applications on a Budget
posts.excerpt: Practical OWASP-aligned habits for startups and freelancers who cannot afford a dedicated security team.
posts.body:
Secure development does not always require expensive tools. It starts with input validation, HTTPS everywhere, and keeping dependencies updated.

Use environment variables for secrets, never commit API keys to GitHub, and test forms for common injection patterns.

These foundations dramatically reduce risk for portfolios, business sites, and client projects.

posts.title: Microsoft 365 users fall victim to one-in-a-million password spray attack
posts.excerpt: The attack exploited previously exposed credentials and flaws in enterprises’ multi-factor authentication configurations
posts.body:
Microsoft users have been hit by a massive, automated password spray attack.

Among those targeted by the attack were clients of security company Huntress. It reported that the attackers made 81 million attempts to log into its customers’ accounts between June 12 and 26 — and succeeded in at least 78 cases.

And that’s just the attacks on Microsoft account holders who also happen to be Huntress customers: The number of compromised accounts could be much higher, as it’s in the nature of a password spray attack to attempt to connect indiscriminately.

The attacks all came from a single source, an IPv6 address range controlled by internet provider LSHIY LLC, Huntress said in a [blog post](https://www.huntress.com/blog/lshiy-password-spray-attack). LSHIY has since terminated access for the customer using the IP addresses involved in the attack.

Huntress had been monitoring spray attacks for some time and had noticed a slight increase from June 12, and then a sudden spike on June 22 when 30 of its customers were affected.

The attackers replayed validated credentials via the [OAuth](https://www.csoonline.com/article/3833826/how-to-configure-oauth-in-microsoft-365-defender-and-keep-your-cloud-secure.html) ROPC (Resource Owner Password Credentials) flow. This takes a username/password at the /token endpoint for a tenant and mints a new user-delegated token, once provided with the correct credentials. This was possible because multi-factor authentication (MFA) had not been configured to handle the techniques deployed by the attackers.

Huntress said that this was because, in some cases, MFA had been enforced for specific apps instead of “All Cloud Apps.” For example, some organizations enforced MFA for Microsoft Admin Portals, which did not cover the Azure CLI logins used by the attacker.

In other cases, organizations enabled MFA only for specific user groups (such as Admins Only). The compromised users were not in the scope of these specific user groups.

Source: [Microsoft 365 users fall victim to one-in-a-million password spray attack | CSO Online](https://www.csoonline.com/article/4192819/microsoft-365-users-fall-victim-to-one-in-a-million-password-spray-attack.html?utm_date=20260704230940&utm_campaign=CSO%20US%20Update&utm_content=slotno-1-readmore-The%20attack%20exploited%20previously%20exposed%20credentials%20and%20flaws%20in%20enterprises%E2%80%99%20multi-factor%20authentication%20configurations.&utm_term=CSO%20US%20Editorial%20Newsletters&utm_medium=email&utm_source=Adestra&aid=29680127&huid=)
